21. New META Andromeda Algorithm Update Creative Diversification Best Practices 8-2-25.mp4 All right, welcome in everybody. Pretty serious update today. So for all those who... didn't see inside of the Telegram chat. We have pinned at the top currently. a message on everything that's happening in relation to all the chaos. of... Meta Andromeda. Meta Andromeda is an update that currently is fucking everybody who does not have diverse creative assets. There is a set of articles on it. The specific article I'd encourage you to read is the one from the engineering department over at Facebook, Engineering at Meta. For all those who don't know, they actually do write technical papers on how everything works on the platform. In this case, let me just be really simple with how I articulate this. Andromeda is the opening first step. That's been updated now. In the ad recommendation process. So simply put, The very first thing that occurs before an ad actually gets distributed is it goes through Andromeda. Okay. So this is really important to understand. Because Andromeda was created for a really simple reason. They articulated in a bunch of nerd talk in the engineering article specifically. There's also a regular one in the Facebook newsroom you can go read that was shared by Daniel, Daniel Walton specifically here in the inner circle. And long story short, the simpler version articulates it obviously in easier to understand terms, but long story short. They... rolled out. Advantage. Audiences, which essentially just makes everything broad targeting a while ago, a long time ago now that they rolled that out. And it's officially every account. Pretty much just doing broad targeting now whenever you do cold audiences. However, they later, it was essentially, I believe, late last year is when they technically started rolling it out to about what appeared like half the accounts. But now it's like pretty much in every single account. They call it 'Advantage Creative.' OK, so 'Advantage Creative.' I'm sure you guys have all seen this. It's pretty fucked. For the most part. In some instances, to be fair, it can be good, but it does a lot of stupid stuff. A lot of the stupid stuff that it does, like, as an example, if you have images, it adds music to every single one of them. It's on by default. You have to go and flick a toggle off. They added what they call 'site links.' Essentially, right below your ads. Now, if you haven't seen that, there's these little squares that show up. They almost look like little square tiles and you can scroll through them, kind of like a carousel ad. Those have been added. And if you don't set them up properly, by the way, they just link to like random shit that is on your website that Facebook can find, some of which might be 404 links, from what we've seen on different brands. Again, you can flick that off if you're doing individual ads. You do not get the ability to flick it off if you're doing dynamic ads. They also have several other random ones that they're still testing. Like there's one that I've seen on half the accounts right now. It's called Promotions. It literally looks like it's like a coupon area where you can add something specific. Anyway, long story short, there's a whole range of them. Some of them include animating images. Some of them include making images 3D. All the ones for videos are a little different. They call it touch-ups, enhanced CTAs. And again, there's about seven to eight of them total, give or take, whether you're doing video ads or whether you're doing image ads. But this— is why they did this, okay? So with Advantage Creative, they wanted to leverage all the generative AI opportunity that everybody's all super fucking hyped about. And long story short, they needed a new first step distributing process in the ad recommendation protocol. Thus, they created Andromeda. So long story short, essentially what they're anticipating with Andromeda is a huge volume of creatives that they previously did not have. They essentially, in their own words, claim that there's upwards of a 10,000x lift in the volume of creatives that can occur based on the amount of new creatives that can happen with advantage creative being turned on and all those little toggles. So again, as a result of that, they needed to hard code something into their platform recommendation process. Again, introducing Andromeda. So Andromeda is really easy to understand. They essentially. because of broad targeting existing, right? So obviously the old way of doing things, we would all target things like demographic traits, interests, and behaviors. In some instances, we would obviously target things like lookalike audiences and even warm audiences. Some accounts don't even necessarily have the ability to target that now. So targeting is pretty much gone. That's all just like a get fucked territory now. Whereas, Broad is the newest, latest, and greatest solution. I actually made a pretty good video about this on YouTube, specifically with the way the distribution of ads is working right now. I'm going to reiterate it real quick. I initially thought the extent of this algorithm update was just to help smaller advertisers. kind of leads behind bigger advertisers who are spending more and better at targeting and reaching people with the right messaging. But simply put, if you're in the algorithm, and you're just sitting there scrolling on Facebook, you'll notice it pretty easily. You'll see content. Content. And then you'll see an ad. And it'll be an ad for, let's say, business type number one. If you don't express interest, again, back to content, content, content, and you're going to get hit with a different type of ad. If you click on any one of these in particular, like let's use the example that you actually clicked on, add to. Again, you go back to the actual scrolling on the platform. You're just going to see content, content, content. more of ad type number two content content content more of ad type number two content content content more ad type number two and, without exaggeration, this specific process repeats upwards of, we're seeing, without exaggeration, probably miles of scrolling. The way that they're doubling down on creative and businesses that are similar to whatever they originally clicked on first and expressed interest in. That's the extent of the initial algorithm update. It really caused a lot of short-term chaos on sales teams. Like sales teams specifically were dealing with the objection of a lead showing up aware of like every single fucking competitor that existed. Whereas previously that wouldn't be the case because those advertisers would have to actually be good at ads. In order to reach people. Nowadays, it's like, Everybody is like that. Little fish on the bottom of a big shark. You know, as soon as a big advertiser reaches somebody and generates the initial click expressing interest, every subsequent advertiser that could possibly be served to that same exact potential lead or buyer is going to get served to them. So again, I just thought that would cause some short-term chaos with sales teams needing to update their, you know, their talks that they would do with people, you know, you just simply put deal with a much more sophisticated lead. That's not that hard to overcome, to be fair. That still happens. I want to be really clear. Saying like, that is still the case. This exact scenario that I just articulated still occurs. But what's, what's specifically rolled out as, as what appears like July of 2025, that's just absolutely fucking people is essentially ad fatigue on steroids. It's not the same thing. It's kind of like. If you had a virus. And, you know, it became like immune to antibiotics. You know, that's essentially the extent of like where we are now. This this is where we are at. It's no longer just duplicate the campaign. throw in some creatives that are pretty much the exact same fucking thing and relaunch it and see the costs come back down again. That specific strategy is nerfed now, it seems. The new way to overcome it requires a lot more effort, give or take. How good you are with diversifying the messaging that you use. So because this specific algorithm is in place now, where, again, when somebody clicks on a specific ad showing any extent of interest and pretty much all targeting is now just broad. You have to understand what Andromeda is looking for, okay? What it's specifically looking for. Is a fuckload of creatives that match. Who it can serve. And add for that's most relevant to that user next. OK, now relevance is historically a big Google AdWords term. This is where relevance has mattered the most historically. Just to give you some context on what I'm referring to, Google AdWords is a pretty simple platform to master if you're not a lazy turd. You have to create ad groups that are relevant to whatever you're targeting. So, like, if I have a bunch of keywords, I always use the example of dog food whenever I teach about this inside of the master internet marketing class. I say, if I wanted to sell dog food, okay? I would want to create ad groups where I can then tell people in my ads and drive them to landing pages. That have congruency across those three different things. I want to create a targeting group of people that are all similar to one another. I want to write ads that are specific to that group of people I'm targeting. And then I want a landing page that's congruent with whatever I said in my ads. Simply put, that's called relevance on Google AdWords. So again, if I search the words 'dog food,' right? And those are the keywords that I'm targeting. And then my ads talk about pet food. That's not relevant. The words 'dog food' being in my ads would make it relevant. And then when they click to my page, I need a landing page in that example that talks specifically and sells dog food. Okay, even if I sell dog toys. Other. Pet food. Pharmaceuticals for animals. Other... pet services. None of that shit needs to be mentioned at all on the page. They simply put, 'they just want relevance across the board.' Now, on Facebook, it's a bit different because we don't need to create targeted lists in ad sets that are all specific to one another because of how the platform works. It's just broad targeting. But what we do need to do is— we need to create a fuckload of ads as though we are attempting to communicate to people's individual reasons to actually buy or do something, okay? This is where most people historically lack. What they'll do is, is they'll have like one main hook. Okay. So I'll use an example of a client right now. We have Cody Sanchez. Her main hook historically is boomers are dying. Somebody needs to take over their businesses. It's one of the greatest wealth transfers in modern history. They're also in a position where they're ready to retire. There's never been an easier time to be able to come in and use creative financing on a deal to buy out a boomer's business and help them retire and take over what they do and get that revenue. Now, that specific hook, you know, we could say it in all kinds of ways, right? But it's essentially no matter how many different ways we say it, relating to boomers or dying, it's a great wealth transfer, those kind of things. It's essentially all the same hook. Okay, it's all really the main thing being said again and again and again, just in like subtly different ways. Okay, those subtle variances are what is currently fucking people. Okay. You can no longer just make subtle variances and have that be all of the ad creative that you have loaded up into the platforms. Okay, it's just not how it works. So comparatively, when they use the words in Andromeda, what you're going to see time and time again, they use the word diversification a lot throughout their ads. OK, so moral of the story, like when you look at whether it's the engineering article or whether it's the one that's meant for regular people on the front end. You can understand very easily what they're technically looking for is a very diverse set of reasons for somebody to be responsive to an ad. OK, so again, I'm just going to keep it super simple. Let's just use dog food. OK, so really think about this. I want to start by saying I'm going to right in the middle. What I'm actually selling, okay, which is dog food in this example, let's move this down a little bit so we can really get dense with the amount of things that we can articulate selling dog food for. So as an example, I'm just going to list a few. Maybe my dog food is healthier. Has anybody seen like the frozen dog food? There's human-grade dog food. Okay, there's quality of ingredients. I'm just going to put quality of food so I don't have to write the word ingredients. Lord knows how to spell that. Okay, in the chat. Be creative. Okay, yeah, good one. Not. highly processed. Okay, now, as an example of what we're doing here, All we're doing is just writing down a fuckload of reasons. that somebody would buy this, okay? That's literally all we're trying to do. Fewer doctor visits. Nice. All right, cool. I think you understand the extent of what I'm attempting to do. I'm attempting to say, okay, what I'm trying to sell. In this case, it's dog food. Okay. Now, the reasons that somebody specifically would want to buy dog food all become my hooks, okay? These all become the things that I can start the ad with. And center the ad around okay one thing that you I'm going to tell you a few things that I'm seeing just get straight up terrible results right now that used to work totally fine. So historically, we can have a hook, a body, a CTA. And these three things being independent from one another, like we could we could interchange some of these things with one another with ease and keep the other part the exact same. So, like, as an example, if I kept my body and my CTA the same and all I did was just rotate my hook specifically. What that would essentially mean is, if I sat down and I filmed creatives, I would only film this part right here once. OK, but then maybe the hook section. Might film like 10 hooks or 15 hooks, right? Now that means I'm only going to need to film the first, like, you know, whatever, five to 30 seconds of the ad, give or take how long the hook part of the ad is. And then my editors are just going to swap out that hook on a fuckload of different body and CTAs that are the exact same. And logically, in that example, I'd have the original ad and then I'd have, you know, 10 more ads or 15 more ads. And I want to be clear when I say. This technically has effectiveness still. But what's better is to literally just film the entire ad all over again. So it's like you'd rather actually film the hook, the entire body and the CTA. Around like one of these specific reasons, and then again when I want to do this, like I want to film the whole thing. Whereas historically, you might just take the hook and and just start the ad with this and this. You can still technically do that. I want to be fair in saying, but what I've seen time and time again in this most recent July month is doing this. It's lasting for a far shorter duration of time and still exposes you to a lot of volatility. Comparatively, just doing an entire unique ad around whatever the reasons are that people are probable to buy. That has a lot longer lifespan, okay. That has a lot longer lifespan, right now, from what we're seeing. So here's the moral of the story. You essentially need to do your own creative best practices here. On your dog food exercise. Like you need to write down. What the main thing is that you're selling, okay? And I want to be fair in saying, some of you might perceptually have more than one main thing that you're selling. That's okay. I think that's actually beneficial because then you'll have more of whatever you want to call this little graph right here in front of us. The moral of the story is, and this is really simple to understand, okay? I'm just going to, I'm going to reiterate it like this. One main hook. Not really working right now. What is working? is Diverse. Messaging. in creatives. Okay. Now there's really two paths you can take. Okay. And the two paths. Honestly, depends on how inherently lazy you might be that determine what path you're actually going to go down here. But regardless, I'm going to articulate both. So path number one: 50 plus creatives a week. Path number two: 15 to 20. diverse, Messaging. unique ads. a month. Okay, so here's what I found in the month of July. So the Andromeda article. Not the one from the engineering department specifically, the other one where it talks about the public-facing one that Daniel shared, that one specifically emphasized a testimonial in it from some e-commerce brand where they literally said, 'We went from three to four creatives a week to 50 a week. While we scaled, and like that maintains stability for us with our results, so that's not bad Right. If you take that specific path. But let me just be clear when I say this. If you do the 50 plus creatives a week, what I've found is you do not need. A wide range of diverse messaging. Okay, I found that if you can churn out 50 plus creatives a week, and essentially you're comfortable with relaunching your campaigns and following ad fatigue protocol, and/ or relaunching your ad sets if you're doing ad set budget optimization every week to overcome ad fatigue. You're not. needing to have a diverse range of messages. Instead, the sheer volume of creatives can help you overcome the existing problem that you have. That's very abnormal for almost every single one of you. And so as a result, you'd obviously have to change a lot about your creative process. For how you're going to be able to execute that. We have a video in our weekly group call library on creative testing best practices. And in that video, I talked very simply about, 'Listen, if you sit down and you film. 10 ads. Okay. And there are 10 different hooks, you know, 10 different bodies. The CTAs I emphasized didn't really matter that much. I can interchange those hooks and bodies with one another and I could easily create 50 ads a week. But the difference there would be I would need to sit down and film 10 ads a week, right? And so it's not tough. But to be clear, it's a much higher quantity of creatives that are necessary to be filmed if you're going to lean into. One main hook. And attempt to optimize around. Less diverse, more hook-centric focused ads. What I'm seeing. And we've talked about this for years, to be clear. I've always emphasized that creative controls the targeting nowadays. So I will emphasize that you will get less reach. If you just have one central hook and are not leveraging a diverse range of reasons, a . k . a. diverse messaging angles, in order to reach more people. Okay. Because again, creative controls targeting right now. That's one thing I want you to burn into your brain when you walk away from this call. Creative controls targeting now, okay? So if you just try to go through sheer volume of more creatives, then technically, yes, that will maintain your costs. That has a high probability to bring down your CPMs. That has a high probability to bring down your cost per result. And just, again, create stability and consistency for you. But I mean, dude, that's a lot. You know, that's 200 creatives a month if it's a month with only four weeks in it. In addition to that, you're going to have to update your creative process. You're going to have to just change a bunch of stuff, which isn't bad. Change is the only constant in life. Comparatively, here's the other set of accounts that we dealt with this month. That didn't deal with any inflation at all. They had very consistent revenue. They did this, you know, where when we sat down and told them, 'Hey, here's what we think you need to film.' They just did it and nothing really changed as a result. So I use Andrew as an example, just to be clear, like Andrew's cost the entire month of July when this rolled out, they didn't fluctuate once. His costs stayed the exact same the entire time. He's getting about $40 to $50 cost per qualified calls. Okay. Spent about $40,000. Okay, nothing crazy, but still. You know, first month together. Did very well on ROAS, you know, consistent leads coming in, consistent types of leads coming in, and more importantly, consistent cost per call, no crazy fluctuations. Here's the point I'm attempting to make when I show you this ad idea document, okay? Andrew said exactly what I wrote in each one of these 15 creatives. OK, my team then took these ads that he sent to us and all we did was just add captions and jump cuts to it. We didn't do any interchanging of anything. We just edited exactly what he sent us because he said exactly what he said. This is important to note, okay? I'm going to demonstrate to you. A diverse range of messaging that all sell the same thing in this case— sober living homes. Okay, so what I want you to specifically look at first is the opening hook. But I don't just want your attention to stay on the hook. I then want you to look at how the body for each one of these justifies the hook that it opens up with. And I want you to think to yourself, like, 'All right, are these truly different? From one another, or do they all sound the exact same?' Okay, so again, let's look at this opening hook. You ever heard of nine people paying rent on one single family home legally and having the government help you fill it? This is the sober living strategy. Not Airbnb, not Section 8, not multifamily. Just one regular house converted into a sober living home. We typically have two to three people per bedroom. Clean, sober, and drug tested. And the rent paid through local agencies, disability, or social security. These folks need a safe space and the government wants to help them find it. Got 13 of these homes right now, and we've installed door cameras legally, hired in-home managers, and built relationships with agencies that consistently send us people and pay us per person. The crazy part? Cashflow beats almost every other real estate model I've seen. You're not depending on tenants with perfect credit or hopping. We're hoping Airbnb isn't banned next year. This is protected housing, serving a mission, and it makes sense. And then the CTAs are all relatively the same across all these, okay? So that's ad number one. Let's go to ad number two. Okay. Hook, let's be real. America has a drug problem. But what happens after someone gets sober? Now let's just look at the differences here before we continue. Add one. You ever heard of nine people paying rent on one single family home legally and having the government help you fill it? Add two. Let's be real. America has a drug problem. What happens after someone gets sober? And now look at the body of this one. OK, again, the body on this one. Fully and totally relates to the hook of 'Add Two.' Okay, the rehab ends. The insurance stops. Most people have nowhere to go. No family. No housing. Just a backpack and hope. That's where sober living homes come in. We provide clean, drug-free homes shared with others. Walking the same path. Everyone agrees to. Random drug testing. Breathalyzers. Curfews and rules. We've got cameras inside and outside. We have a living manager. And the best part, the government often helps pay the rent. We're not only helping people stay clean, we're creating massive cash flow for investors. These homes can generate three to five more monthly profit than a typical rental and they're protected under housing laws that prioritize these target populations. Okay. Now, let's go to ad three. Okay. If you're putting 20% down on a rental right now and hoping it cash flows, you're probably losing money. Bang! Like you see, I'm just going to take a moment. Put a one in the chat if you could recognize how different these are from one another. Okay. You see how this is what diversification means. It's super simple to understand. It is not complex. Okay? It is not complex at all. These are all different reasons that somebody would be responsive to these ads. You understand? Okay, again. I'm not going to read every single one of these. We're just going to lightly go through the hook. And then I'm going to share this document with you after. You can do what you'd like with it. Okay. Again, look at the hook. If you're putting 20% down and then you best believe, obviously, the copy. And the body here. resonates with that opening hook. Look at how little it actually talks about the sober living homes here. Interest rates are 7%. Rents are capped. And let's be honest, renters can leave you hanging. That's why— Transition to sober living homes. I don't rent to one family. I rent by the bed. Nine people, one house, $800 per bed. That's $7,200 a month gross on a property that might rent for $2,400 a month traditionally. You see what I'm saying? So again, my point being, This... Specifically, this level of diversification that you see. Okay this is what protected this account from this exposure this month. Every account that we have comparatively. That did the same exact type of messaging. Okay. Suffered. Even with volume. They couldn't keep up with 50 a week. They didn't have the ability to shoot 10 new ads a week for us to turn them into 50 ads. Okay. They need, I prefer path two, simply put. I prefer path two. I think path two is way better. Okay? I think path two is far easier to justify. Because guess what? Andrew, you're on the call now. Can you unmute yourself? How many ads did I ask you to film in the month of July? Are you talking? I think you unmuted yourself, but it sounded like you were super far away or something. Maybe covered your mic. I don't know. My bad. It was 15 and then another 15. Yeah, bang. And we didn't even use the second 15. We asked him for another 15 for the month of August. Because he's going on a trip. So long story short, we had him film 15 ads. And guess what? We only used 10 of those ads. Throughout the entire month of July, because we didn't have to rotate them, because they maintained the same cost per call throughout that entire month. Again, we always believe in a creative surplus, especially with this Andromeda update. So long story short, I currently have 20 ads just sitting there in the surplus. Ready to go for when we inevitably see regular ad fatigue. So at this point, I just want to make a quick note. Regular ad fatigue still exists in its normal form. When you have overcome this problem, okay, if you if you follow path two specifically, and you go down the path. of a nice diverse range of creative assets. Okay. You have truly diverse messaging. You're just going to deal with regular ad fatigue. OK, now, if you go down path number one, where you use the same fucking hook and you just make 50 creatives, you are going to deal with the version of ad fatigue that's on steroids that became immune to the traditional protocol. OK, no longer can you just relaunch an ad that's got like fucking three creatives in it with three new creatives and see it lower in cost per result. If you do not have diverse messaging, you are going to get absolutely fucked on with this new update. Okay, that's the moral of the story. So anyway, back to this. Let's just look at the opening hooks again, okay? Do you know how many seniors we've housed who are literally sleeping in Walmart parking lots? Next one. One of the craziest things about this real estate model is that you don't need a license. You don't need a certification. Next one. Worried about damage when you hear shared housing? Let me show you how we solved that. Next one, 15% of the U. S. population is in recovery from drugs or alcohol. And most people have no idea sober living homes even exist. next one wants to know why local agencies consistently send people to our sober living homes. Again, you see what I'm saying here? Every one of these is different. They're all actually unique. But they follow the dog food analogy. Right. It's like. We're selling dog food. But we're talking in the ads about unique, totally unique reasons. Okay, now here's the cool part. It's like technically. what you're used to. is what we call a majority hook. Okay, a majority hook has historically been rather hard to find. Because most of you don't actually diversify the amount of messaging that you've put into ads. You haven't actually gone to the extent that you now need to not to get fucked on. And now you're going to have to. And so now you actually have a much higher probability to find one specific hook that is actually your majority hook. Okay, and you're obviously per usual gonna still see more reach on that hook compared to the other ones. You're probably to see more life in that specific hook and more longevity in that specific hook compared to the other ones. But that in no way, shape, or form negates the need to have a diverse range of messaging in every single set of ads that you launch from here forward. You understand? Okay, now there's also a very important distinction I want to make clear. There are completely different best practices and protocols. relative to these two different paths. OK, so I'm going to list a few of them off that I believe are necessary for you to understand. So, 50 plus creatives a week. Number one. You can. Swap hooks. and keep the same. bodies and CTAs. Okay, that can easily get you to 50. You absolutely can. Add B-roll. To the same ads you've already launched. And technically they count as new ads. And that can help you overcome it. You can. leverage dynamic ads. To make this a little easier. In creating the variations, because again, if you have 10 vids or images, and you have. Five headlines. And you have five. Pieces of body copy. That combination right there is 250 total variations that can get created with dynamics. Okay. I just want to be clear on this. There are some downsides to dynamic, officially, whereas historically there really weren't a lot. Dynamic downsides now are pretty fucked. So number one, the site link extensions. Number two. Adding the shittiest music to image ads I've ever heard. Very off-brand. And then all Advantage. Plus creative toggles are on. Okay, when you use dynamic, you are... the guinea pig. Okay, you are the test dummy. They can at any point just turn on all the shit that they want to roll out on creative advantage. Or I should say, advantage creative toggles onto your dynamic ads. So I want to be clear when I say this. Some accounts, like, they don't have any of this dumb ass shit turned on when you use dynamic ads. In other accounts, it's like. Every dumbass possible combination of creative advantage stuff is on. And it just looks like shit. Then it feels off brand and it converts worse. So let me be clear when I say this. The other disadvantage... of doing the 50 plus creatives a week. Is your ad campaign requiring you might have to do individual ads? Okay. And if you do individual ads, it obviously requires you to just do a lot more work. That's really all it is. So it's like, if you have to launch, picture this, right? A campaign ad set with 50 ads in it. Each specific one of those 50 has a unique ad. Okay, you can technically have the dynamic. Uh, turned on in a very subtle way by still putting multiple copy variations with each creative, but like 50 total ads that you need to technically run. Are going to require you to again have 50 individual ads that all have a different individual thing. Now the reason historically I want to be fair in saying, like this really isn't that much effort, but the reason it's more effort than ever before with running individual ads is they added this like six-step process per individual ad that you have to go through now. So like, when you go create an individual ad, whether it's an image or a video now, the very first thing it asks you is, is, 'Which image or video do you want to select?' Then it immediately tries to toggle. on the promotions or site links or whatever other one might be on your account, then from there it has you confirm your copy okay. Then from there it has you confirm whether you want all the other advantage creative toggles turned on or off. Which give or take, whether it's a video or an image, are going to require you to toggle what you don't want turned on or off. And that can be up to eight individual toggles that you have to manually select, or sometimes as little as five. Then from there you do a final comp. Sometimes, by the way, if it's an image, it'll then prompt you with a step where it makes generative ads for you. That give or take, the product can look fine, but for a lot of you guys, for what you specifically sell, it's going to look like shit. And you're going to press skip on that, or you're going to wait for it to create the shitty images and then you'll Press skip and then you'll do a final confirmation and then you'll then you'll officially have your ad done. Then you'll duplicate that change the creative on it and have to go through those same six steps again. 50 times over. So simply put, just to put it in perspective with basic math. If you had to do that, you know, 50 times over with those six individual things, you're going through those same steps 300 times weekly. So in a month now, with, again, four weeks in a month. You're going through that step 1,200 times. Okay. In terms of. 50 times the 6. times the four. Okay. So you have to do that 1,200 fucking times a month. If you have to do that. Okay? And. I will tell you, from experience of doing this for over a decade, it is the longest, most drawn-out ad creative launch process I have experienced to date. It is exhausting. It's taken something that historically was like 10 to 15 minutes to do. And it's become like a fucking two-hour task. Okay? So just to be clear, I have not found a more efficient way to do it yet. It's just what it is right now. It just takes that amount of time from what I've experienced. So I'm in no way, shape, or form trying to make it sound like it's not possible to do. It's just simply put. This takes a fuckload of effort now. Like a fuckload. Okay. Not just in the, not just in the ad campaign creation process either. Obviously, it takes a fuckload of effort too, with just having to up the amount of creatives that you're going to produce on a week to week basis. It's more for your editing team. It's more money that you have to pay out if you're doing a paper editor model or a paper video model, I should say. And then, in addition to that, obviously, you just now got to justify the time to sit down and film like all these fucking ads every single week. So. Anyway... That's that path. Okay. And then you got this other path. Which, as I mentioned, is my preferred path, okay? If I'm not working with a retard, like I'm having him take this specific path, okay? This path is far easier. Very simple. They typically. Once... to twice a month. are gonna film About 15 videos. Okay. Those 15 videos, they're all going to be like what I showed you here. But as an example, it's like, remember, the same logic of how hooks work applies here. Okay, we've talked about this in the majority hook mastery video, but just to reiterate, typically there is a majority hook. Okay, there is some variation of a hook out there that's going to get the most response, have the most consistency for you, generate the cheapest cost per result, generate the highest ROAS. And then, from there, you start working into these minority hooks, where, as an example, example like this might be your second greatest hook that you have and it technically works just as well, but you know, not for as many people and it dies out a little faster. And then, historically, I've articulated to you that you have all these little minority hooks and these minority hooks, like, typically, when you find them, they're not even necessarily worth like playing into is what I've historically told you. Whereas, nowadays, like. So here's here's how it's actually going to look for you, just to put it in perspective. Right. Like, you're still going to have this here where you have your majority hook defined and you're still going to have this here where you have your second hook defined. But! You ideally, in terms of this specific quadrant, for the minority hooks, you want a fuckload of minority hooks. Like a fuck club. This is specifically where I think Manis does best in helping go super niche and granular on. Things that you just might not regularly think of off the top of your head. Okay. Historically, the minority hooks, just to be clear, are something you'd want to stay clear of. Like a great example of this, just to point it out in like a very technical way. If we look at these Andrew Lam ads, and we specifically see some of these, I think one example of a true minority hook is this one here, Ad4. Do you know how many seniors we've housed who are literally sleeping in Walmart parking lots? I don't believe that that hook is going to have mass appeal. To an investor, it's like, 'Who the fuck wants to?' You know, put your balls on the table and invest in a property to house seniors living in a Walmart parking lot. You know, it doesn't sound like you're going to be getting cash flow off of that type of demographic. So I generally historically would consider that a minority hook. Right. And I wouldn't actually tell Andrew to go film that with this new paradigm that exists with Mint, with Andromeda, and the need for creative diversification. I have more of these than ever before. I actually have more minority hooks being filmed. Comparatively to more of the majority and second-order hooks than I ever have before. Like out of my 15 ads, 13 of them are going to be minority hooks. So, although I want to be very clear in saying that. Yes, you might think like, okay, well, if I film minority hooks, like that's technically one third of my overall demographic that's going to drive the revenue or that's going to be responsible. To the ads and historically, like in the old paradigm, the old way that things work, like yeah, you'd technically be right, we'd all be right in thinking that, in this new and drama paradigm, the minority hooks are what help get the targeting accomplished, reach more people before seeing fatigue occur, and maintain stability in the creative process. Process where we see consistent cost per result and lower cost per result. That's been one of the most interesting things I really want to point out: that's kind of changed a little bit about the majority hook now. That we've historically done. I still want you to be able to find your majority hooks. But again, I want you to remember, minority hooks are essentially... what buys stability and longevity in the accounts currently from what I'm seeing. That's the biggest update that I can emphasize to you here in regards to all of this when you look at creative diversification. Don't just think to yourself, it needs to be... the biggest and best hooks. Don't think that at all. It's actually the exact opposite. Like what we did up here with the dog food analogy was a perfect example. Like Charles saying something about, 'hey, you're'— dogs aren't going to have shitty farts anymore. Like they're not going to be terrible. I would honestly film that ad. I would have that client film that ad as silly as it is, dude, that would probably work well at keeping our call. Slow—I would film every single one of these. I would work with mana specifically on attempting to create even more of these. I would film every single one of them. Am I still going to find some hooks that better than the others? Yes. Am I still going to find some hooks that get better ROAS than the others? Yes. Am I still going to maintain a lower cost per result for a longer duration of time as a result of having more diverse creatives? Yes. Is that what you technically want? Yes. Would it reach investors because the algorithm scans the text upfront, or why would it reach investors? Why wouldn't it? It talks about it in the entire second half of the ad. You're sitting here thinking to yourself that the opening hook is what's going to dictate the targeting and not the entirety of the ad and the marketing process. Yeah. That's retarded. Absolutely not. The ad is scanned in its entirety. In its entirety, it's consumed by the algorithm. The page that you drive the traffic to in its entirety is consumed by a crawler. Okay. And then from there, they determine. Who's probable to get targeted by it. So again, the words that are spoken in the ad, the words that are written in the copy, every single thing that's spoken on your page and written on your page, that's what dictates the targeting. They already know it's for investors. This specific ad would reach investors that also care about America's drug problem. You understand? Now I understand. So all the copy that's on the page and in the ad is not just for the consumer, the viewer, for the first five seconds, but also for the algorithm who uses it. uses it for targeting. That's exactly right. So let me give you a comparison so you can also think with this as a great example. If I used image ads that had very little copy on it, if I used short form copy in my body text, if I use short copy on my landing pages. I'm probably to have a bad time. Yeah, that explains a lot. Evolutionary psychology. The book talks about. As human beings, we used to not have unlimited calories. We used to have to go out and literally hunt and kill animals. Haul them back to our village. and break down the animal to eat it and then we'd have to go do it again when we wanted more food. Okay, and we obviously all the women and just gatherers would have to go and gather food as well. The point being, nowadays we live in a society where we have unlimited calories, okay? We have unlimited energy. We literally have unlimited energy. There is absolutely no need in any way, shape or form to be inherently lazy. OK, there's no reason to do that. Your brain by default wants to be inherently lazy for energy conservation. Because again, the way that we were all gotten to this point in life before modern society was energy conservation. We laid around most of the day until we needed to go hunt and get more calories. Energy was not unlimited. Nowday, you genuinely have unlimited energy. Even if you make no money, the government will take care of you. And you will have unlimited energy. Okay. Point being. Will, can you unmute yourself and ask whatever you're trying to ask? Yeah, so I'm saying, with all the new creatives that you're doing, like if you're testing with Option One, are you still kind of doing a broad CBO with uh different kinds of dynamic packs and then individual ads within there, then taking winners and then scaling them into like an ABO? Uh, depends on— So this specific account, there are... Excuse me, let's go last 30 days. Two campaigns here that are running. These two campaigns, the cold campaign has three ad sets: lookalike, stack broad, and an interest stack. Pretty much all broad targeting to be fair, but you know, whatever. And then the warm campaign has the warm interest stack. In this case, I put them all to CBO and then I did dynamic. What I'm also finding is historically, and I'll talk about this in the fifth cohort of the Master Internet Marketing class later this month that we do. The historical emphasis on needing to do like campaign variations to see significant difference in cost per result was very low. And this, like modern paradigm, it has much more emphasis. So simply put, like if I'm not seeing great results with one particular campaign structure, I will change the campaign structure. So by default. I may not bias towards CBO. I may bias towards ABO and dynamic. If that doesn't work, then I'll relaunch the campaign. I'll probably leave ABO on and do individual ads. You see what I mean? Like I'm very willing to change. The type of campaign structure I have in this modern paradigm and seeing successful results with it. One person that has messaged me back and forth quite frequently that has an agency in the group, Evan Seach. And Evan has... He's been talking me up a wall about that same thing where he's like, historically, this has meant nothing to have to change the campaign structure to see any kind of change. But now it's like that's one of the number one things that can immediately create change. And it's also technically— a lower effort action— where you don't have to go and think that, oh, I got to change the creatives or I got to change this or that. So sometimes, when you see a bottleneck in the process and it relates specifically to the ads, I am actually at a much higher weighted rate than historical averages, changing campaign structure as a first primary action to see differences with that bottleneck. Whereas historically, that would be very low on my list, comparatively. Does that make sense? Yeah, it does. Okay. Thank you. Very good. No problem. Okay, Daniel says, Are you setting up campaigns definitely without drama? Daniel, does that answer your question, what I just said there? Yeah, pretty much. And I'm curious if you've seen this too, like in some of the accounts, we're noticing like leads or like prospects getting locked in on certain campaigns and not moving over to other campaigns. Have you noticed that? I'm not sure what you mean by that. Can you be a little more articulate? Yeah, so like one campaign you can check, like on like Hyros or whatever uh attribution software you're using, and you'll see like the whole journey they won't like somebody won't actually leave the campaign. So, pretty much, if you had any campaigns split between like top of funnel, middle of funnel, bottom of funnel, or just any campaign variation. Like the journey really locks in on one campaign and they're just rotating them through whatever creatives you have set up in that campaign. I understand what you mean. Yeah, we haven't done that. Top of funnel, middle of funnel, bottle of funnel shit, like literally ever. We've just always put every single one of those types of creatives into a dynamic campaign and just allowed it to do exactly what you just articulated. So that's been for us. If that has not been normal for you, then to be clear, yeah, you can just throw literally every single type of creative within a campaign. And to your point, it will rotate through the creatives. One thing that I've seen, I can't remember who it was, but somebody was messaging me bitching about frequency going up. I think it was Eli. He was like, 'Oh dude, like my frequency goes up like super fast on a campaign.' And I don't view that as a bad thing at all. You know, if ROAS is maintaining, if your cost per result is maintaining and you're seeing frequency go higher, that is a great example of the effect of that. Because more creatives are getting served to an unconverted individual than ever before. You're going to see your frequency shoot a little higher because it typically circles around the people who are more probable to convert. And then it leeches off to the people or fatigues after it goes through that cycle. So to your point, yeah, I've definitely been seeing that for sure. Okay. And then, just to be, to be clear. So what you're saying is like, if you do dynamic and it's not working, like relaunch with the other structure and that relaunch with individual ads in that example, and also consider the diversification as well. I mean, one thing I want to point out. Obviously, you have to make sure you have a diverse range of creatives to see success in this current environment. So any specific account where they have, let's say, 10 of the exact same sounding ad. They're having a really bad time, comparatively to the people that just launched with 10 ads that have super diverse messages where they all sound totally different from one another. That's the other main thing I really want to make sure I emphasize— like campaign structure is just higher on our list of changes that we're still seeing make an impact and definitely get better results. But... The main, like, overarching thing that we want to ensure we have, if we have diverse creatives already in place, we'll do a campaign change. If not, we're just going to go tell a client, like, 'Hey, man, we need you to get some diverse creatives in place here in order for us to see success.' It's not just going to be a campaign structure change that makes the difference. We might test that in the meantime while we're waiting for the client to film. Yeah, the diverse assets are what really make the difference there. Does that make sense? Yeah, it does. Thank you. All right, cool. No problem. Okay. Does anybody else have questions? What you can do to control where spend goes. But in no way, shape or form do you want to be thinking like that. That is one of the worst ways you could think. The better way to think is, okay, in this new paradigm, and again, you missed the whole first half of the call. So you need to rewatch the recording when we go through this. The whole thing that you have to understand about Andromeda is. It wants creative diversification, which obviously is going to require you to have more minority hooks than you've ever had before. And as a result of you having more minority hooks than you've ever had before, you're more probable in this current paradigm to experience lower cost per result for longer durations of time as a result of that. So it's like, it's not a question that should be a block of, 'okay, well, how do I prevent spend going to a majority?' It's like, no, no, you want spend going towards majority. minority hooks in this example. You won't spend going towards all of these things because that's what's probable to create longevity. That's what's probable to create lower cost per result in this new environment relative to this algo update. Does that make sense? Yeah, yeah. Comparatively now, it's like. The exact opposite. It's like all the little dinky hooks that historically would never really do much at all are actually pulling a lot of fucking weight right now, which I think is cool.
